data.title: In November, expect America to follow its moral compass
data.description:
After Kamala Harris lost the presidency in 2024, political wunderkind James Carville wrote, “It was, it is, and it always will be the economy, stupid.” 

Inflation and the high cost of living were on voters’ minds in 2024. That said, more factors go into their decision-making than calculating how much money they have left in their wallets. For example, whether a candidate shares their values is less tangible than counting dollars but hugely important.

In my book “The New Politics of Old Values,” I wrote that when Ronald Reagan won in 1980, Americans were shaken by a belief that the country had lost its moral footing. I wrote back then that 71 percent believed that “many things our parents stood for are going to ruin right before our eyes,” nearly eight in 10 thought “what is lacking in the world today is the old kind of friendship that lasted for a lifetime” and half agreed with the statement, “I feel left out of things going on around me.”

Today, many Americans feel the same way. A 2023 report issued by then Surgeon General Vivek Murthy described an “epidemic” of loneliness and isolation. One respondent told Murthy, “If I disappear tomorrow, no one will even notice.”

For Reagan, the presidency was a place where the values of family, work, neighborhood, peace and freedom could be redeemed. Reagan’s pollster, Richard Wirthlin, advised his client, “The primary leadership function of the American president is to reaffirm constantly the country’s highest purposes and the potential for individual efforts to alter the course of the future in a positive direction.”

Today, Reagan’s new politics of old values has been replaced by Trump’s dedication to exacting retribution from his perceived enemies and deporting migrants who he believes are “poisoning the blood of our country.”

The killing of Renee Good by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement agent has been shown countless times on television screens and social media feeds and has awakened the national conscience. Minnesota Gov. Tim Walz (D) denounced the “organized brutality” of ICE, while Trump countered that a “DAY OF RECKONING & RETRIBUTION IS COMING!”  

Polling after Good’s death finds the Trump presidency in a downward spiral. An AP-NORC poll finds just 38 percent endorse Trump’s handling of immigration. And when it comes to the economy — another Trump signature issue — only 37 percent approve. A CNN poll finds just 33 percent believe Trump cares about people like them, and 58 percent believe Trump’s presidency is a failure.

Cruelty, chaos and corruption are the hallmarks of the Trump administration. And there is a growing chorus of voices reacting against all three. In Portland, Ore., Catholic Archbishop Alexander Sample writes that Trump’s immigration crackdown is an affront to the “human dignity” that comes from God. Welcoming the stranger is not just a central tenet of Christianity, but fundamental to being a good, decent neighbor.

Empathy is making a comeback. U.S. Senate candidate and state Rep. James Talarico (D) wrote on X, “Some politicians say they want a ‘Christian Nation’ until it comes to providing healthcare for the sick, funding food assistance for the hungry, raising the minimum wage for the poor. They want to base our laws on the Bible until they read the words of Jesus.”

On the other side of the political spectrum, Joe Rogan, the popular podcaster who endorsed Trump in 2024, describes ICE agents as “militarized groups of people roaming the streets” and asks, “Are we really going to be the Gestapo? Is that what we’ve come to?” Not surprisingly, only 35 percent say they are proud to have Trump as president. 

Decades ago, Vaclav Havel, the Czech playwright and poet who became his country’s president, declared that politics, morality and civility were intertwined, writing. “Those who find themselves in politics bear a heightened responsibility for the moral state of society,” he wrote. “And it is their responsibility to seek out the best in that society, and to develop and strengthen it.” 

While Havel acknowledged that “the vain, the brash, and the vulgar … are drawn to politics … if your heart is in the right place and you have good taste, not only will you pass muster in politics, you are destined for it.” 

For his part, Havel described himself as “an instrument of the time.” In choosing the next president, voters will select someone to be our instrument who will write the next chapter in our history books. By 2028, a yearning for stability and reclaiming our lost values will give us a president who has little in common with Trump.

John Kenneth White is a professor emeritus at The Catholic University of America. His latest book is titled “Grand Old Unraveling: The Republican Party, Donald Trump, and the Rise of Authoritarianism.”

data.title: Trump says US used 'Discombobulator' secret weapon during Maduro capture
data.description:
President Trump said U.S. forces used a secret weapon called the “Discombobulator” that helped impair Venezuelan equipment during the early January raid in Caracas that led to the capture of Venezuelan leader Nicolas Maduro. 

“The Discombobulator. I’m not allowed to talk about it,” the president said in an interview with The New York Post, which was published Saturday. 

“I would love to,” Trump said, before discussing the effectiveness of the mysterious weapon used during the operation on Jan. 3, where Maduro and his wife, Cilia Flores, were arrested at a compound in Caracas by U.S. personnel. 

“They never got their rockets off. They had Russian and Chinese rockets, and they never got one off,” the president told The Post. “We came in, they pressed buttons and nothing worked. They were all set for us.”

U.S. personnel used cyber tools during “Operation Absolute Resolve,” knocking out power across the capital city. There have also been reports promoted by the White House that personnel tasked with defending Maduro were incapacitated during the attack.

The U.S. military possesses a directed-energy weapon, called the Active Denial System, that shoots an invisible beam of radio frequency, which penetrates to the depth of roughly three sheets of printer paper. 

“This repel effect produces an intolerable heating sensation, compelling the targeted individual to instinctively move,” the Pentagon has said previously. 

It is unclear which exact weapon Trump was referring to during the interview.

data.title: WSJ editorial board: Trump administration 'spin' on Alex Pretti 'simply isn't believable'
data.description:
The editorial board of the Wall Street Journal is pushing back on the federal government’s account of the shooting death of Alex Pretti during a confrontation with immigration authorities in Minneapolis over the weekend.

The Journal, in an editorial published on Sunday, called the incident “the worst … to date in what is becoming a moral and political debacle for the Trump Presidency.”

“Videos of an event aren’t always definitive, but this is how it looks to us,” the newspaper wrote. “Pretti attempted, foolishly, to assist a woman who had been pepper-sprayed by agents. Multiple agents then tackled Pretti, and he had a phone in one hand as he lay on the ground. An agent discovered a concealed gun on Pretti, and disarmed him. An agent then shot Pretti, and multiple shots followed.”

Top White House official Stephen Miller called Pretti “a domestic terrorist,” while federal immigration leaders have defended his shooting death.

“He was a nurse without a criminal record,” the Journal wrote, pushing back on Miller. “Pretti made a tragic mistake by interfering with ICE agents, but that warranted arrest, not a death sentence. The agents may say they felt threatened, but it’s worth noting the comments over the weekend by police around the country who say that this isn’t how they conduct law enforcement.”

The newspaper knocked Miller and Department of Homeland Security chief Kristi Noem, saying they aren’t “credible spokespeople” on the rising tensions in Minnesota, writing their “strategy is to own the libs, rather than to persuade Americans.”

“Mr. Miller’s mass deportation methods are turning immigration, an issue Mr. Trump owned in 2024, into a political liability for Republicans in 2026,” it concluded. “Americans don’t want law enforcement shooting people in the street or arresting five-year-old boys. The President who said you have to have a heart in enforcement ought to show some.”

data.title: Venezuela acting president says she's had 'enough' of orders from Washington
data.description:
Venezuela’s Acting President Delcy Rodríguez said she has had enough of orders from Washington, in a speech to a group of oil workers broadcast on Venezuela’s state-run television on Sunday. 

Rodríguez’s remarks come a little over three weeks since President Trump announced the capture of Venezuela’s President Nicolas Maduro in an audacious military raid in Caracas. He has since declared that the U.S. would take over the country’s oil industry and dictate orders to the government. 

“Enough already of Washington’s orders over politicians in Venezuela,” Rodríguez told a group of oil workers in Puerto La Cruz city, CNN reported. 

“Let Venezuelan politics resolve our differences and our internal conflicts. This Republic has paid a very high price for having to confront the consequences of fascism and extremism in our country.”

Trump greenlit Rodríguez staying in her role after the capture and transfer of Maduro to New York to stand trial for criminal drug charges. 

Trump told reporters last week that Rodríguez had “shown very strong leadership so far,” “had done a very good job,” and was working with the U.S. to move millions of barrels of oil “into the United States.” 

“We’re working with them and they’ll get some of that, and we’re going to get some of that,” Trump said aboard Air Force One on his way back from the World Economic Forum in Davos.

“And it’ll be divided up and our country will become richer and that means our taxes are going to be going down and, and they will do better. Venezuela’s going to do better than they’ve ever done. Even with us, you know, taking a, a very fair piece of it.”

Trump has held back on calling for new elections in Venezuela but has warmed up to opposition leader and Nobel peace prize laureate María Corina Machado after initially saying she didn’t have support in the country.

Trump met with Machado on Jan. 15 in the White House, where she presented the president with her Nobel Peace Prize, an honor that the president has openly coveted.

data.title: Live updates: Immigration crackdown lands in Minnesota court as Trump sends Homan there
data.description:
Another deadly shooting by federal immigration enforcement officials in Minneapolis has shaken the country, and it may push Washington to shut down part of the government later this week.

The killing of 37-year-old Alex Pretti by a Customs and Border Patrol agent on Saturday has renewed uproar nationally, and Senate Democrats have said they will vote against the Department of Homeland Security (DHS) funding package as Jan. 30 shutdown deadline looms.

Some Republican lawmakers are joining Democrats in calling for an impartial investigation into Pretti’s shooting.

A federal judge in Minneapolis will hear arguments Monday morning as the state of Minnesota sues the Trump administration, saying its immigration raid tactics are unconstitutional.

President Trump said Monday morning he would send border czar Tom Homan to Minnesota to begin managing Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) actions there.

On Capitol Hill this week, the House is out, but the Senate is in, and it must pass spending bills to avert a government shutdown.

Trump on Monday will hold policy meetings and do a radio interview. Press secretary Karoline Leavitt will brief reporters at 1 p.m.

Follow live below.

data.title: Trump on closing his eyes during Cabinet meetings: 'Boring as hell'
data.description:
President Trump said he’s closed his eyes during Cabinet meetings because they are “boring as hell” but noted this isn’t a reflection of his health. 

“It’s boring as hell, I’m going around a room, and I’ve got 28 guys — the last one was three and a half hours. I have to sit back and listen, and I move my hand so that people will know I’m listening,” Trump told New York Magazine.

“I’m hearing every word, and I can’t wait to get out,” he added.

In recent months, speculation about the president’s ability to deal with chronic venous insufficiency and lead the country by past staffers, political strategists and the public has mounted.  

Several people described the president as “incoherent” while giving remarks at Quantico last October, and others, including conservative commentator Megyn Kelly even said he was “rambling” on the campaign trail and displayed “senior moments.” 

But those in Trump’s orbit defended Trump’s behavior and noted his innate ability to notice details both small and large in a split second.

“The guy is too healthy. He’s too active,” Secretary of State Marco Rubio told New York Magazine, detailing one time when Trump surprised him with a set of medallion samples after noticing some were missing from chandeliers inside the State Department.

The secretary of state said when the White House leader closes his eyes, it’s a “listening mechanism” that tunes speakers in rather than drowning them out. 

Amid support from one of his top Cabinet officials, the president says he regrets taking an MRI scan and heeding the advice of his medical professionals at Walter Reed this fall, as it caused more questions about the state of his health. 

“It was the worst f—— thing I ever did, and I blame them.” Trump pointing at his doctors. 

“They wanted me to take it. And because I took it, people want to say, ‘Oh, there must be something wrong,’” he added.

However, the president has vehemently defended himself to the press and continues to clamp down on narratives proposing that his health is deteriorating.

“I got an MRI. It was perfect,” Trump previously told reporters. “I mean, I gave you the full results. We had an MRI and the machine, you know, the whole thing, and it was perfect.”

Critics teased him for stating he had “no idea” what part of the body was scanned.

“It was just an MRI,” he said. “What part of the body? It wasn’t the brain because I took a cognitive test, and I aced it.”

Colonel James Jones, who serves in the White House’s medical unit and sat in on Trump’s interview with the magazine said they took a “computerized tomography exam of his chest and his abdomen,” noting the president didn’t actually have an MRI but something “MRI-like.”

“The reason for the imaging, as routine as we stated, is that any patient his age could have things, and we ruled them out,” said Jones. “The story should be about the fact that the results were, uh, perfect. They did not demonstrate any problems.”

data.title: More Democrats call for Noem impeachment
data.description:
More Democrats are pushing for the impeachment of Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem following another fatal shooting by federal immigration authorities in Minneapolis.

“Kristi Noem and her department’s latest attempt to mislead the American public regarding the brutal and unjustified killing of Alex Pretti is deeply shameful, and she must be impeached and removed from office immediately,” Sen. Jacky Rosen (D-Nev.) said in a Sunday statement.

Alex Pretti, a 37-year-old intensive care nurse who lived in Minneapolis, was fatally shot Saturday by a Customs and Border Patrol (CBP) agent. The fatal shooting came a few weeks after an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer fatally shot Renee Good in the same city.

Both of the shootings have sparked massive backlash over President Trump’s immigration agenda, which is losing support even within Trump’s base.

In her own Sunday statement, Rep. Laura Gillen (D-N.Y.) said “[a]nother U.S. citizen has been killed at the hands of ICE and there must be accountability, which is why Secretary Noem must be impeached immediately.”

“Under her leadership, ICE has targeted U.S. citizens and children and killed Americans. She is not focused on safety or border security; she’s focused on chaos and self-promotion,” the New York Democrat added.

Rep. Ilhan Omar (D-Minn.) said Noem’s impeachment was among the “bare minimum” steps in response to the shootings.

“Voting NO on the DHS funding bill is the bare minimum. Backing Kristi Noem’s impeachment is the bare minimum. Holding law-breaking ICE agents legally accountable is the bare minimum. ICE is beyond reform. Abolish it,” she wrote in a Sunday post on X.

Voting NO on the DHS funding bill is the bare minimum. Backing Kristi Noem’s impeachment is the bare minimum. Holding law-breaking ICE agents legally accountable is the bare minimum. ICE is beyond reform. Abolish it.”

Rep. John Larson (D-Conn.) wrote in a post on X that Noem has “baselessly” labelled Good a domestic terrorist after her shooting.

“Now, agents killed another resident on her watch. I don’t take impeachment lightly, but this moment demands action. Americans deserve leaders who put them above this authoritarian agenda,” he wrote.

The Democratic Party’s official account on X posted an all caps push for Noem’s impeachment on Sunday afternoon, reading “IMPEACH KRISTI NOEM.”

Earlier this month, Rep. Robin Kelly (D-Ill.) introduced articles of impeachment against Noem, pointing to the Homeland Security secretary’s work on the Trump administration’s immigration crackdown.

“Secretary Noem has brought her reign of terror to the Chicagoland area, L.A., New Orleans, Charlotte, Durham, and communities north to south to east to west,” Kelly said during a press conference. “She needs to be held accountable for her actions.”

The Hill has reached out to the Department of Homeland Security for comment.

data.title: Ye apologizes for anti-Semitic remarks: 'I lost touch with reality'
data.description:
Ye, the rapper formerly known as Kanye West, is issuing an apology for his anti-Semitic remarks and past embrace of the Nazi swastika, saying mental health challenges caused him to lose “touch with reality.”

Taking out a full page ad in The Wall Street Journal on Monday, Ye penned a lengthy letter directed “to those I’ve hurt.”

“Twenty-five years ago, I was in a car accident that broke my jaw and caused injury to the right frontal lobe of my brain,” Ye wrote, citing the 2002 incident that preceded his debut hit, “Through the Wire.”

“The deeper injury, the one inside my skull, went unnoticed,” the 48-year-old entertainer said, writing that the “possibility of a frontal-lobe injury was never raised.”

Ye said he was eventually diagnosed in 2023, but the “medical oversight caused serious damage to my mental health and led to my bipolar type-1 diagnosis.”

Bipolar disorder, Ye said, makes “you feel like you’re seeing the world more clearly than ever, when in reality you’re losing your grip entirely.”

In recent years, Ye has made frequent highly inflammatory antisemitic remarks.

In a 2022 interview, he praised Adolf Hitler, saying he “liked” the Nazi leader. 

That same year, his X account was temporarily suspended after he posted that he was “going death con 3 on JEWISH PEOPLE.” He faced suspension again that December after sharing an image of the Star of David containing a swastika inside.

Last year, Shopify shuttered Ye’s online store that was linked to his website, which was selling $20 T-shirts bearing large swastika symbols. 

In his Wall Street Journal letter, Ye said during that time he “lost touch with reality.”

“In that fractured state, I gravitated toward the most destructive symbol I could find, the swastika,” Ye said. 

“I regret and am deeply mortified by my actions in that state, and am committed to accountability treatment, and meaningful change. It does not excuse that I did though. I am not a Nazi or an antisemite. I love Jewish people,” the musical artist said.

“To the black community — which held me down through all of the highs and lows and the darkest of times. The black community is, unquestionably, the foundation of who I am. I am sorry to have let you down. I love us.”

Ye said after “hitting rock bottom a few months ago,” his wife, Bianca Censori, urged him to seek help. He said he’s since found an “effective regime of medication, therapy, exercise, and clean living,” along with “much-needed clarity.”

Ye, who’s supported President Trump and launched a long-shot presidential bid in 2020, vowed to pour his energy into “positive, meaningful art: music, clothing, design, and other new ideas to help the world.”

“I’m not asking for sympathy, or a free pass, though I aspire to earn your forgiveness,” Ye, who has four children with ex-wife Kim Kardashian, said. 

“I write today simply to ask for your patience and understanding as I find my way home.”

data.title: Trump wants to be king of the world with his 'Board of Peace' stealth attack
data.description:
That President Trump thinks of himself as the world’s dominant figure is hardly a secret. Last April, he told two interviewers that, in his first term, “I had two things to do — run the country and survive.” His second term agenda? “I run the country and the world.”  

As the Atlantic’s Ashley Parker and Michael Sherer explain, the president “has always been convinced of his own genius, his pure gut instincts. But never more so than today. The past four years have turned him into a Nietzschean cliche. Banishment, multiple indictments, a 34-count felony conviction, repeated brushes with assassins — all have combined to convince him that he is impervious to challenges that would destroy others.”

Earlier this month, Trump reaffirmed the belief in his global indispensability when he told The New York Times that the only limit on his powers is “My own morality. My own mind. It’s the only thing that can stop me.”

And last week, the president made news by announcing the executive committee of his so-called Board of Peace and holding a ceremony to launch the board at the World Economic Conference in Davos, Switzerland.

The Board of Peace was originally conceived to enlist international involvement in implementing the U.S. plan to reconstruct Gaza. Members of its executive committee will include billionaires Steve Witkoff and Marc Rowan; Trump son-in-law Jared Kushner; Secretary of State Marco Rubio and U.S. national security adviser Robert Gabriel, members of Trump’s political inner circle; President of the World Bank Group Ajay Banga; and former British Prime Minister Tony Blair.

But no one should be fooled. The Board of Peace, under Trump’s chairmanship, is a device to enlist others in ratifying his belief that he and only he can run the world. If he has his way, the board will act in ways the United Nations — for 80 years the world’s most important international organization — cannot.

Trump’s vision and ambition are clear from the Board of Peace charter. 

Its preamble speaks in universal terms. It declares that “durable peace requires pragmatic judgment, common-sense solutions, and the courage to depart from approaches and institutions that have too often failed. … That only sustained, results-oriented partnership, grounded in shared burdens and commitments, can secure peace in places where it has for too long proven elusive.” 

Whereas the U.N. endorsed a focus on Gaza, the Board of Peace’s new statement of purpose says nothing about it. Instead, it calls the Board “an international organization that seeks to promote stability, restore dependable and lawful governance, and secure enduring peace in areas affected or threatened by conflict.”

Not since Woodrow Wilson conceived of a League of Nations in the wake of World War I has a single figure imagined himself capable of reordering the world. But, unlike Wilson’s design for the League, which contemplated shared decision-making, Trump’s Board of Peace ratifies his desire to run the world in accordance with his own morality and his own mind.

Among other things, the charter says that Trump himself “shall serve as inaugural chairman of the Board of Peace, and he shall separately serve as inaugural representative of the United States of America. … The chairman shall have exclusive authority to create, modify, or dissolve subsidiary entities as necessary or appropriate to fulfill the Board of Peace’s mission.” It specifies that decisions of the board “shall be made by a majority of the member states present and voting, subject to the approval of the chairman.” 

The world may vote, in other words, but Trump decides. 

The rollout of the Board of Peace evokes the unscripted drama, high-stakes gamesmanship and suspense of a television reality show. It turns international relations into a strange spectacle in which formal invitations to world leaders alternate with Trump’s speeches mocking them. 

And let’s not forget that, for a $1 billion contribution, members of the Board of Peace can secure permanent membership (as opposed to a three-year appointment, which requires no contribution). And membership is not exactly voluntary, as French President Emmanuel Macron found out on Jan. 19 when he expressed concerns that the Trump-chaired body would have extensive powers beyond transitional governance of the Gaza Strip and would undermine and seek to replace the United Nations framework. In response, Trump threatened a 200 percent tariff on French wines. 

Reuters quotes a diplomatic source who agrees with Macron that the Board of Peace is a “‘Trump United Nations’ that ignores the fundamentals of the U.N. Charter.”  

Alas, it is the U.N.’s commitment to “fundamental human rights, in the dignity and worth of the human person, in the equal rights of men and women and of nations large and small, and to establish conditions under which justice and respect for the obligations arising from treaties and other sources of international law” that are incompatible with Trump’s desire to be the world’s supreme leader.  

Austin Sarat is the William Nelson Cromwell Professor of Jurisprudence and Political Science at Amherst College. Ruxandra Paul is Assistant Professor of Political Science at Amherst College and an affiliate of the Minda de Gunzburg Center for European Studies at Harvard University.

data.title: Blanche says nobody thinks Alex Pretti's actions meet 'legal definition of domestic terrorism'
data.description:
Deputy Attorney General Todd Blanche said Monday that the actions of Alex Pretti before he was fatally shot Saturday by a Border Patrol agent in Minneapolis did not constitute domestic terrorism.

“I don’t think anybody thinks that they were comparing what happened on Saturday to the legal definition of domestic terrorism,” Blanche said on Fox News’s “Fox and Friends.”

“What we saw was a very violent alteraction and I am not going to pre-judge the facts,” he added.

In the aftermath of the shooting, Homeland Security Secretary Kristi Noem said that Pretti, a 37-year-old U.S. citizen, committed domestic terrorism and brandished a weapon at Customs and Border Protection (CBP) agents.

“When you perpetuate violence against a government because of ideological reasons and for reasons to resist and perpetuate violence, that is the definiton of domestic terrorism,” Noem told reporters Saturday. “This individual who came with weapons and ammunition to stop a law enforcement operation of federal law enforcement officers committed an act of domestic terrorism.”

But video of the incident shows Pretti recording agents with his phone as they pushed a woman to the ground, upon which he attempted to assist her. CBP personnel then surrounded Pretti, with one appearing to seize a firearm from his waist before an agent fired multiple rounds.

Minneapolis Police Chief Brian O’Hara told reporters Saturday that Pretti was a lawful gun owner with a permit to carry. Minnesota law also does not restrict individuals from carrying a firearm at a protest.

Earlier this month, Noem also characterized 37-year-old Renee Good, who was fatally shot by an Immigration and Customs Enforcement (ICE) officer in Minneapolis, as a “domestic terrorist.” She claimed that Good attempted to hit ICE officer Jonathan Ross with her car, causing him to fire in self-defense.

Video of that incident shows Good backing up, turning her wheel and driving forward, with Ross firing shots after he was out of the way of the vehicle. 

Federal law defines domestic terrorism as criminal activities intended to intimate or coerce civilians, influence government policy via intimidation or coercion or impact government conduct by mass destruction, assassination or kidnapping.
